1:
"And Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain, and said, I have gotten a man from the LORD." The witnesses note the birth of Cain and Eve's words. On "Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain," the grammatical voices note the first birth. Ellicott and Gill note that "Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain" — the first human birth, the firstborn of mankind; the propagation of the race begins; Eve, the mother of all living (3:20), bears her first son, Cain (whose name relates to "gotten" or "acquired").

On "I have gotten a man from the LORD," interpreters note Eve's words. The reformed Calvin and Gill note that Eve's exclamation, "I have gotten a man from the LORD," expresses her sense of God's gift and perhaps her hope in the promise (3:15); some read it as a hope (mistaken) that Cain was the promised seed who would bruise the serpent (the words being rendered by some "I have gotten a man, the LORD," or "a man from the LORD"); whether or not she thought Cain the Messiah, she acknowledges the child as God's gift and lays hold of the promise.

On "a man from the LORD," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the acknowledgment: Eve confesses that the child comes "from the LORD" — the gift of God; her words mingle gratitude with hope; in the birth of her son she sees God's faithfulness and the continuance of the race through which the promised seed would come; the believing recognition of God's hand in the gift of children. The verse displays the birth of Cain and Eve's words.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the birth — "Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain," the first human birth, the propagation of the race (Ellicott, Gill); "I have gotten a man from the LORD," Eve's acknowledgment of God's gift, perhaps her hope in the promise (3:15), some reading a Messianic hope (Calvin, Gill); the believing recognition of God's hand (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Cain's birth. The verse marks the birth: of Cain. The verse displays the mother: Eve. The verse displays the words: from the LORD. The verse displays the hope: in the promise. The birth displays the first child acknowledged as God's gift. The verse displays the Cain: born. The verse displays the gift: from God. The verse displays the hope: of the seed. The birth of Cain and Eve's words display the beginning of the race: Adam knew Eve, and she bore Cain, saying, "I have gotten a man from the LORD" — the first human birth, Eve acknowledging the child as God's gift, perhaps with the hope of the promised seed. The verse displays the firstborn Cain, with his brother Abel (v.2).
2:
"And she again bare his brother Abel. And Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground." The brief verse notes the birth of Abel and the brothers' callings. The grammatical voices note the second son. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "she again bare his brother Abel" — Eve bears a second son, Abel (whose name means "vapor" or "breath," perhaps foreshadowing his brief life); the two brothers, Cain and Abel, the first of the race born of woman.

On "Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground," the witnesses note the callings. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abel was a keeper of sheep (a shepherd), but Cain was a tiller of the ground (a farmer)" — the brothers follow different occupations: Abel a herdsman, Cain a cultivator; both honest callings, the two basic forms of early human labor (pastoral and agricultural); the division of labor in the first family.

On the callings, the reformed Calvin notes the providence: Calvin and Gill note that the brothers' different occupations were both lawful and honorable; God provides for human life through such labors; the difference in calling is not the ground of God's later regard (vv.4-5), which rested on faith and the heart, not the occupation; both shepherd and farmer may serve God. The verse displays the birth of Abel and the brothers' callings.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second son — "she again bare his brother Abel," Eve bearing Abel, the second son (Keil, Ellicott); "Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground," the brothers' different honest callings, pastoral and agricultural (Ellicott, Gill); both lawful, the difference not the ground of God's regard (Calvin, Gill). The verse displays the two brothers. The verse marks the birth: of Abel. The verse displays the callings: shepherd and farmer. The verse displays the labor: honest. The verse displays the family: divided in work. The birth displays the two brothers in their callings. The verse displays the Abel: born. The verse displays the sheep: kept. The verse displays the ground: tilled. The birth of Abel and the brothers' callings display the first family's labors: Eve bore Abel, who became a shepherd, while Cain was a farmer — the two brothers in their honest, different callings, both lawful before God. The verse displays the brothers, who bring their offerings (vv.3-4).
3:
"And in process of time it came to pass, that Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering unto the LORD." The witnesses note Cain bringing an offering of the fruit of the ground. On "in process of time it came to pass," the grammatical voices note the occasion. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "in process of time (at the end of days, perhaps an appointed time) it came to pass, that Cain brought... an offering unto the LORD" — at some appointed or customary time, the brothers brought offerings to God; the bringing of offerings shows that the worship of God by sacrifice was already practiced; man approached God with gifts.

On "Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering unto the LORD," the witnesses note Cain's offering. Henry and Gill note that "Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering" — Cain, the farmer, brought of his produce, the fruit of the ground; the offering was of the kind suited to his calling; whether the kind of offering or the heart behind it was at fault is considered in vv.4-5; Cain's offering was a bloodless gift of the ground's produce.

On "an offering unto the LORD," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the worship: the bringing of an "offering (minchah, a gift) unto the LORD" shows that sacrifice and the worship of God were established; men acknowledged God and brought him offerings; the practice of offering points to the recognition of God's claim and (in the right offering) of man's need; the question of acceptance (vv.4-5) turns on the offerer and the offering. The verse displays Cain bringing an offering of the fruit of the ground.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the occasion — "in process of time... Cain brought... an offering unto the LORD," the brothers bringing offerings at an appointed time, the worship of God by sacrifice already practiced (Keil, Gill); "Cain brought of the fruit of the ground," Cain's bloodless offering of his produce (Henry, Gill); "an offering unto the LORD," the established worship of God by offering (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Cain's offering. The verse marks the time: in process of time. The verse displays the offering: fruit of the ground. The verse displays the offerer: Cain. The verse displays the worship: by sacrifice. The offering displays Cain bringing his produce to God. The verse displays the Cain: offering. The verse displays the fruit: of the ground. The verse displays the LORD: worshiped. Cain bringing an offering of the fruit of the ground displays the first recorded worship: in process of time Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering to the LORD — the worship of God by sacrifice already practiced, Cain bringing his bloodless produce. The verse displays Cain's offering, set beside Abel's (v.4).
4:
"And Abel, he also brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof. And the LORD had respect unto Abel and to his offering:" The witnesses note Abel's accepted offering — the crux of the ground of acceptance. On "Abel... brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof," the grammatical voices note Abel's offering. Clarke and Gill note that "Abel... brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof" — Abel, the shepherd, brought the firstlings (the firstborn lambs) and their fat; an animal sacrifice, the best of his flock; the firstlings, frequently used in sacrifice, a fitting type of Christ (the slain Lamb); Abel's offering involved the death of an animal, the shedding of blood.

On "the LORD had respect unto Abel and to his offering," interpreters expound the ground of acceptance. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and the reformed Gill stress faith and the atoning sacrifice: by faith Abel offered "a more excellent (or fuller, pleiona) sacrifice" (Hebrews 11:4); professing faith in the promised Messiah, feeling his need of atonement, Abel offered a vicarious, bloody sacrifice (the firstling, prefiguring Christ); the kind of offering (a blood-sacrifice, owning sin and atonement) marks his faith. The reformed Calvin stresses the person: God "had respect unto Abel" first, then to his offering — the offerer's faith and heart were accepted before the gift; God looks first to the man, then to his sacrifice; the difference lay in faith and the heart, not merely the matter. Most join the two: Abel's faith, expressed in the right, atoning sacrifice, was accepted.

On "had respect unto Abel and to his offering," the reformed JFB and grammatical-historical note the regard: God showed his acceptance (perhaps by fire from heaven consuming the sacrifice); Abel and his offering were accepted, in contrast to Cain (v.5); the acceptance rested on faith and the fitting, atoning sacrifice. The verse displays Abel's accepted offering.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abel brought the firstlings of his flock, a blood-sacrifice, and the LORD accepted him and his offering (Clarke, Gill, Calvin, JFB). The ground of acceptance bears a crux: Abel's faith (Hebrews 11:4) and his heart, the person accepted before the gift (Calvin), and/or the kind of offering — a blood/atoning sacrifice prefiguring Christ, vs Cain's bloodless fruit (Clarke); most join faith and the fitting sacrifice. The verse displays the acceptance crux. The verse marks the offering: the firstlings. The verse displays the debate: faith, heart, or the sacrifice. The verse displays the respect: to Abel. The verse displays the type: of Christ. The acceptance displays Abel and his offering received by God. The verse displays the Abel: accepted. The verse displays the firstling: offered. The verse displays the faith: shown. Abel's accepted offering displays the ground of acceptance and a crux: Abel brought the firstlings and fat, and the LORD had respect to him and his offering — read as the acceptance of his faith and heart (the person before the gift, Calvin) and of his atoning blood-sacrifice (Clarke, Hebrews 11:4), in contrast to Cain. The verse displays the accepted Abel, against the rejected Cain (v.5).
5:
"But unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect. And Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell." The witnesses note the rejection of Cain's offering and his wrath. On "unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect," the grammatical voices note the rejection. Ellicott and Gill note that "unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect" — God did not accept Cain or his offering; the rejection was of the person and his gift; Cain, lacking faith (Hebrews 11:4) and bringing a bloodless offering without the sense of sin and atonement, was not accepted; the contrast with Abel (v.4) is pointed.

On "Cain was very wroth," the reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Clarke note the anger. Calvin and Clarke note that "Cain was very wroth (greatly angry)" — instead of repenting and amending, Cain was filled with wrath; his anger was directed against God (who rejected him) and against Abel (who was accepted); the sin of envy and rage rises in his heart; the rejection, which should have led to repentance, kindled wrath.

On "his countenance fell," the puritan and grammatical-historical note the fallen face: "his countenance fell" — Cain's face fell, betraying his inward anger, envy, and dejection; the outward sign of the sullen, wrathful heart; the fallen countenance marks the beginning of the path that leads to murder; God's question (v.6) addresses this very anger and dejection. The verse displays the rejection of Cain's offering and his wrath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the rejection — "unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect," God not accepting Cain or his bloodless offering, for lack of faith (Hebrews 11:4, Ellicott, Gill); "Cain was very wroth," Cain's anger against God and Abel, envy and rage instead of repentance (Calvin, Clarke); "his countenance fell," the outward sign of the wrathful, sullen heart (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wrathful Cain. The verse marks the rejection: of Cain. The verse displays the wrath: very great. The verse displays the envy: of Abel. The verse displays the countenance: fallen. The rejection displays Cain angry and dejected at God's refusal. The verse displays the Cain: rejected. The verse displays the wrath: kindled. The verse displays the face: fallen. The rejection of Cain's offering and his wrath display the contrast with Abel: God had no respect to Cain or his offering, and Cain was very angry and his countenance fell — the rejection, met not with repentance but with wrath and envy, the fallen face betraying the sullen heart. The verse displays the wrathful Cain, questioned by God (v.6).
6:
"And the LORD said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen?" The brief verse notes God questioning Cain's anger. The grammatical voices note the gracious inquiry. Gill and Henry note that "the LORD said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen?" — God, in mercy, questions Cain about his anger and dejection; the question is a gracious expostulation, meant to bring Cain to reflect on the unreasonableness of his wrath and to turn him from the path of sin; God reasons with the angry man before he sins further.

On "Why art thou wroth?", the reformed Calvin notes the rebuke and the call. Calvin and Gill note that God's "Why art thou wroth?" is both a rebuke of Cain's groundless anger and a call to self-examination; Cain's wrath was unjust (against God for a just rejection, and against his innocent brother); the question invites Cain to consider and amend; God deals patiently with the sinner.

On "why is thy countenance fallen?", the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the appeal: God notes Cain's fallen countenance, the sign of his sullen, envious heart; the question presses him to examine the cause of his dejection — not God's injustice, but his own fault; the remedy (v.7) lies in doing well; God's gracious question seeks to forestall the coming sin. The verse displays God questioning Cain's anger.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inquiry — "the LORD said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen?", God's gracious expostulation, meant to turn Cain from sin (Gill, Henry); "Why art thou wroth?", the rebuke of groundless anger and the call to self-examination (Calvin, Gill); "why is thy countenance fallen?", the appeal pressing Cain to consider his own fault (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the questioned Cain. The verse marks the question: why wroth. The verse displays the inquiry: gracious. The verse displays the rebuke: of anger. The verse displays the call: to amend. The inquiry displays God reasoning with the angry Cain. The verse displays the LORD: questioning. The verse displays the wrath: rebuked. The verse displays the countenance: noted. God questioning Cain's anger displays the gracious dealing with the sinner: the LORD asked Cain why he was angry and why his countenance had fallen — a merciful expostulation, rebuking his groundless wrath and calling him to self-examination, to forestall the coming sin. The verse displays the questioned Cain, instructed in the way of acceptance (v.7).
7:
"If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted? and if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over him." The witnesses note God's warning to Cain — the crux of "sin lieth at the door." On "If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted?", the grammatical voices note the way of acceptance. Gill and Ellicott note that "If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted (lifted up)?" — God sets before Cain the way of acceptance: if he does well (with faith and a right heart, offering rightly), he will be accepted, his fallen countenance lifted up; the rejection is not arbitrary, but the remedy is in Cain's own doing well; God holds out hope of acceptance on amendment.

On "if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door," interpreters divide over the difficult phrase. The grammatical-historical Keil and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke read "sin (chattath) lieth (croucheth, as a beast of prey) at the door" — sin pictured as a wild beast crouching at the door, ready to spring on Cain if he does not well; the danger of sin lying in wait. A second reading takes chattath as "a sin-offering" — "a sin-offering lieth (coucheth) at the door": even now, a sin-offering is available to Cain at hand, if he will use it; the way of atonement is open. The two readings turn on whether chattath means "sin" or "sin-offering."

On "unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over him," interpreters again divide. The grammatical-historical Keil reads this of sin: sin's desire is toward Cain (to master him), but he ought to rule over it (the same words as 3:16, applied to sin). The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads it of Abel: "these words are not spoken of sin, but [of Abel]" — Abel's desire (deference) shall be to Cain as the elder, and Cain shall rule over him; Cain need not envy, for his birthright stands. The verse displays God's warning to Cain.

The witnesses agree the substance: God sets before Cain the way of acceptance (doing well), and warns of the danger if he does not — sin (or a sin-offering) is at the door, and the matter of desire and rule (sin's, or Abel's) is set before him (Gill, Keil, Clarke, Ellicott). The difficult phrases bear cruxes: "sin lieth at the door" — sin as a crouching beast (Keil) vs a sin-offering at hand (the sin-offering reading); and "unto thee shall be his desire" — referring to sin (which Cain must master, Keil) vs to Abel (the deferring younger brother, Clarke). The verse displays the "sin at the door" crux. The verse marks the way: do well. The verse displays the warning: sin at the door. The verse displays the debate: sin or sin-offering; sin or Abel. The verse displays the rule: over it. The warning displays God setting acceptance and danger before Cain. The verse displays the well-doing: rewarded. The verse displays the sin: crouching. The verse displays the rule: urged. God's warning to Cain displays the way of acceptance and a crux: if Cain does well he will be accepted, but if not, sin lies at the door — read as a crouching beast whose desire is toward Cain, which he must rule (Keil), or a sin-offering at hand, the desire and rule referring to Abel (Clarke). The verse displays the warned Cain, who slays his brother (v.8).
8:
"And Cain talked with Abel his brother: and it came to pass, when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him." The witnesses note the first murder — Cain slaying Abel. On "Cain talked with Abel his brother: and it came to pass, when they were in the field," the grammatical voices note the deed in the field. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Cain talked with Abel his brother: and... when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him" — Cain, harboring envy and wrath (vv.5-7), drew his brother into the field (away from witnesses) and there killed him; the first murder, premeditated and treacherous; the wrath that God warned against (v.7) issues in fratricide.

On "Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the gravity. Henry and Calvin note that Cain "rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him" — the murder of a brother, the righteous by the wicked (1 John 3:12, "Cain... slew his brother. And wherefore? Because his own works were evil, and his brother's righteous"); the first death is a murder, and a fratricide; the fruit of envy, unbelief, and unmastered sin; the enmity of the serpent's seed against the woman's (3:15) breaks out in blood.

On the murder, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the warning unheeded: Cain did not master the sin crouching at the door (v.7), but let it master him; the unrepented wrath grew into murder; the dreadful example of sin unchecked; Abel, the first martyr, falls by his brother's hand. The verse displays the first murder — Cain slaying Abel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the deed — "Cain... when they were in the field... rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him," Cain drawing Abel into the field and killing him, the first murder, premeditated (Keil, Gill); "rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him," the fratricide of the righteous by the wicked (1 John 3:12, Henry, Calvin); the warning unheeded, sin unmastered issuing in murder (v.7, Clarke). The verse displays the murder. The verse marks the field: the place. The verse displays the rising: against Abel. The verse displays the slaying: of his brother. The verse displays the sin: unmastered. The deed displays Cain murdering his righteous brother. The verse displays the Cain: murdering. The verse displays the Abel: slain. The verse displays the blood: shed. The first murder — Cain slaying Abel — displays the fruit of unmastered sin: Cain drew his brother into the field and killed him — the first death a fratricide, the righteous slain by the wicked (1 John 3:12), the wrath God warned against (v.7) breaking out in blood. The verse displays the murdered Abel, whose blood God seeks (vv.9-10).
9:
"And the LORD said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother? And he said, I know not: Am I my brother's keeper?" The witnesses note God's question and Cain's hardened denial. On "the LORD said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother?", the grammatical voices note the searching question. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "the LORD said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother?" — God questions Cain, as he questioned Adam (3:9); not of ignorance, but to bring Cain to confession and conviction; the question presses the murderer to account for his brother; God gives Cain opportunity to confess.

On "I know not," the reformed Calvin notes the lie. Calvin and Gill note that Cain answers, "I know not" — a brazen lie added to the murder; Cain denies knowledge of his brother's whereabouts, hardening himself in his sin; the murderer adds falsehood to bloodshed; the impenitence and hardness of Cain, who will not confess.

On "Am I my brother's keeper?", the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the insolent denial: Cain's "Am I my brother's keeper?" is a rude, insolent rejection of responsibility — denying any duty toward his brother; the callous, defiant words of the hardened sinner; instead of repenting, Cain disowns all concern for Abel; the cold-heartedness of the murderer, who casts off both truth and brotherly duty; the contrast with God's care for the slain. The verse displays God's question and Cain's hardened denial.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the question — "the LORD said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother?", God's searching question to bring Cain to confession (3:9, Keil, Henry); "I know not," the brazen lie added to the murder (Calvin, Gill); "Am I my brother's keeper?", the insolent denial of responsibility, the hardness of the murderer (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hardened Cain. The verse marks the question: where is Abel. The verse displays the lie: I know not. The verse displays the insolence: my brother's keeper. The verse displays the hardness: impenitent. The denial displays Cain lying and disowning his brother. The verse displays the LORD: questioning. The verse displays the Cain: lying. The verse displays the brother: disowned. God's question and Cain's hardened denial display the impenitence of the murderer: God asked where Abel was, and Cain lied — "I know not" — and insolently disowned all duty, "Am I my brother's keeper?" — adding falsehood and callousness to bloodshed. The verse displays the lying Cain, confronted with the blood crying out (v.10).
10:
"And he said, What hast thou done? the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground." The witnesses note Abel's blood crying out from the ground. On "What hast thou done?", the grammatical voices note the divine charge. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "What hast thou done?" — God now charges Cain directly; the question is an exclamation of horror and a charge of the dreadful deed; God, who knows all, confronts Cain with the enormity of his sin; the murder cannot be hidden from God.

On "the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the crying blood. Calvin and JFB note that "the voice of thy brother's blood (bloods, in the plural) crieth unto me from the ground" — Abel's shed blood cries out to God for vengeance; the blood, soaked into the ground, cries from the earth; the figure of the murdered innocent crying to God for justice (Hebrews 12:24, the blood of Abel; Revelation 6:10); God hears the cry of the oppressed and avenges blood.

On the cry, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the witness against Cain: the crying blood is the witness against the murderer; though Cain denied and lied (v.9), the blood itself testifies; God is the avenger of blood; the innocent blood cries out, and God answers with judgment (vv.11-12); no murder is hidden from God, and innocent blood calls for justice. The verse displays Abel's blood crying out from the ground.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "What hast thou done?", God's horror and charge of the dreadful deed (Keil, Gill); "the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground," Abel's shed blood crying to God for vengeance (Hebrews 12:24; Revelation 6:10, Calvin, JFB); the crying blood the witness against the murderer, God the avenger of blood (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the crying blood. The verse marks the charge: what hast thou done. The verse displays the blood: crying. The verse displays the cry: for vengeance. The verse displays the witness: against Cain. The charge displays the innocent blood crying to God against the murderer. The verse displays the deed: charged. The verse displays the blood: crying. The verse displays the justice: sought. Abel's blood crying out from the ground displays God's charge and the cry for justice: God said, "What hast thou done? the voice of thy brother's blood cries to me from the ground" — the murdered innocent's blood crying to God for vengeance, the witness against Cain that no lie could silence. The verse displays the crying blood, answered by the curse on Cain (vv.11-12).
11:
"And now art thou cursed from the earth, which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand;" The brief verse notes the curse on Cain from the earth. The grammatical voices note the sentence. Gill and Calvin note that "now art thou cursed from the earth, which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand" — Cain is cursed; the curse comes "from the earth," which had received Abel's blood; the very ground that drank the innocent blood becomes the instrument or sphere of Cain's curse; the murderer is cursed in relation to the earth.

On "cursed from the earth," the witnesses note the personal curse. Calvin and Gill note that, whereas the ground was cursed for Adam's sin (3:17), now Cain himself is cursed — a heavier curse on the person of the murderer; Cain is cursed from (or by) the earth, which will no longer yield to him (v.12); the curse falls personally on Cain for his crime.

On "which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the figure: the earth "opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand" — the ground, as it were, drank the blood Cain shed; the blood from Cain's hand soaked into the earth; the figure marks both the enormity of the crime (the earth defiled with innocent blood) and the ground of the curse (the earth, defiled by Cain, turns against him). The verse displays the curse on Cain from the earth.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the curse — "now art thou cursed from the earth," Cain personally cursed, a heavier curse than Adam's (Gill, Calvin); "cursed from the earth," the murderer cursed in relation to the ground that received the blood (Calvin, Gill); "which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand," the earth drinking the innocent blood Cain shed (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the cursed Cain. The verse marks the curse: from the earth. The verse displays the murderer: cursed. The verse displays the earth: drinking the blood. The verse displays the crime: enormous. The sentence displays Cain cursed by the defiled earth. The verse displays the Cain: cursed. The verse displays the blood: received. The verse displays the ground: turned against him. The curse on Cain from the earth displays the sentence on the murderer: Cain is cursed from the earth, which opened her mouth to receive his brother's blood from his hand — the murderer personally cursed, the very ground defiled by the innocent blood now turning against him. The verse displays the cursed Cain, the earth withholding its strength (v.12).
12:
"When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength; a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth." The witnesses note Cain's sentence — the barren ground and the wandering life. On "When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength," the grammatical voices note the barren ground. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength" — the curse strikes Cain's calling (he was a tiller of the ground, v.2): the ground will no longer yield its strength (its full produce) to him; his labor will be frustrated, the earth barren to him; the murderer's toil is cursed.

On "it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength," the reformed Calvin notes the frustration. Calvin and Gill note that the ground's refusal to yield its strength is a special curse on Cain's labor — his farming, already under the general curse (3:17-19), is now made fruitless; the earth will not reward his toil; the murderer is punished in the very calling he followed.

On "a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the wandering: "a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth" — Cain is condemned to a restless, wandering, unsettled life; driven from place to place, without rest or settled home; the figure of the guilty conscience and the outcast life of the murderer; Cain becomes a homeless wanderer, marked by his crime. The verse displays Cain's sentence — the barren ground and the wandering life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the barren ground — "it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength," the ground refusing its produce to Cain, his labor frustrated (Keil, Gill); the curse striking his calling, the earth fruitless to him (Calvin, Gill); "a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth," Cain condemned to a restless, wandering, outcast life (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sentenced Cain. The verse marks the ground: barren to him. The verse displays the labor: frustrated. The verse displays the life: wandering. The verse displays the outcast: fugitive. The sentence displays Cain cursed in his labor and condemned to wander. The verse displays the ground: withholding. The verse displays the Cain: fugitive. The verse displays the rest: denied. Cain's sentence — the barren ground and the wandering life — displays the punishment of the murderer: the ground will not yield its strength to Cain, and he shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth — his labor cursed and his life made a restless, homeless wandering. The verse displays the sentenced Cain, who despairs of his punishment (v.13).
13:
"And Cain said unto the LORD, My punishment is greater than I can bear." The witnesses note Cain's despairing complaint — with a translation crux. On "My punishment is greater than I can bear," interpreters note the rendering. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and the reformed Gill note that the words may be rendered "My punishment is greater than I can bear" (the common reading) or "My iniquity is greater than can be forgiven" (an alternative rendering, the Hebrew avon meaning both "iniquity" and "punishment"); on the first reading, Cain complains of the severity of his sentence (despair, not repentance); on the second, he despairs of forgiveness (a confession, but a despairing one).

On "My punishment is greater than I can bear," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the despair. Henry and Calvin note that Cain's words, on the common reading, express not penitence but despairing complaint — he feels the weight of his punishment but does not truly repent; the sorrow of the world, not godly sorrow; Cain laments his misery, not his sin; the despair of the hardened, who feel the punishment but not the guilt.

On the rendering "iniquity... greater than can be forgiven," the grammatical-historical note the despair of forgiveness: on this reading, Cain despairs of pardon — his guilt too great to be forgiven; whether read as complaint of punishment or despair of forgiveness, Cain's words lack true repentance; he does not seek mercy but bemoans his lot; the contrast with the penitent who casts himself on God's mercy. The verse displays Cain's despairing complaint.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the despair — "My punishment is greater than I can bear," Cain's despairing complaint, not true repentance (Henry, Calvin); the rendering may be "punishment... greater than I can bear" or "iniquity... greater than can be forgiven" (avon = both, Clarke, Gill); whether complaint of punishment or despair of pardon, the words lack true repentance (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the despairing Cain. The verse marks the complaint: my punishment. The verse displays the rendering: punishment or iniquity. The verse displays the despair: not repentance. The verse displays the burden: too great. The complaint displays Cain bemoaning his lot without true repentance. The verse displays the Cain: despairing. The verse displays the punishment: felt. The verse displays the repentance: lacking. Cain's despairing complaint displays the sorrow of the world and a crux: Cain said his punishment was greater than he could bear — rendered also "my iniquity is greater than can be forgiven"; whether complaint of punishment or despair of pardon, his words bemoan his misery without true repentance. The verse displays the despairing Cain, fearing for his life (v.14).
14:
"Behold, thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the earth; and from thy face shall I be hid; and I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth; and it shall come to pass, that every one that findeth me shall slay me." The witnesses note Cain's lament over his fate and fear of being slain. On "thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the earth; and from thy face shall I be hid," the grammatical voices note the exile. Gill and Clarke note that "thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the earth; and from thy face shall I be hid" — Cain laments that he is driven out from his settled place and (more grievously) from God's presence and favor; the exile from the face of the earth (his land) and from God's face (his fellowship and the place of worship); the alienation of the murderer from God and home.

On "I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth," the witnesses note the wandering. Gill and Calvin note that Cain owns his sentence (v.12) — a restless, wandering, homeless life; he feels the misery of the outcast lot; the punishment weighs on him.

On "every one that findeth me shall slay me," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the fear: "every one that findeth me shall slay me" — Cain fears that, as a wandering outcast, he will be killed by any who find him (avengers of Abel's blood, or others); the guilty conscience dreads retribution; the murderer fears the very violence he committed; the terror of the guilty, haunted by the fear of death. The verse displays Cain's lament over his fate and fear of being slain.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exile — "thou hast driven me out... and from thy face shall I be hid," Cain's exile from his land and from God's presence (Gill, Clarke); "I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond," the misery of the wandering outcast (Gill, Calvin); "every one that findeth me shall slay me," Cain's fear of being killed, the terror of the guilty conscience (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fearful Cain. The verse marks the exile: driven out. The verse displays the hiding: from God's face. The verse displays the wandering: fugitive. The verse displays the fear: of being slain. The lament displays Cain dreading his outcast, endangered fate. The verse displays the Cain: exiled. The verse displays the face: hidden from God. The verse displays the death: feared. Cain's lament over his fate and fear of being slain display the misery of the murderer: Cain bewailed being driven out from the earth and from God's face, a fugitive and vagabond, fearing that any who found him would kill him — the alienation and terror of the guilty conscience. The verse displays the fearful Cain, given a protecting mark (v.15).
15:
"And the LORD said unto him, Therefore whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold. And the LORD set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him." The witnesses note the sevenfold vengeance threatened and the mark of Cain. On "whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold," the grammatical voices note the protection. Gill and Ellicott note that "whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold" — God, in answer to Cain's fear (v.14), declares that whoever kills Cain will be punished sevenfold; God protects the life even of the murderer from private vengeance; the threat restrains any from killing Cain; the divine prerogative over life and vengeance is asserted.

On the sevenfold vengeance, the reformed Calvin notes the restraint. Calvin and Gill note that God's sevenfold threat is an act of restraint and (in a sense) mercy — Cain is spared immediate death and protected from being slain; God reserves vengeance to himself; the murderer is preserved alive, to live out his cursed, wandering life as a warning; God's forbearance even toward the guilty.

On "the LORD set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the mark: "the LORD set a mark (a sign) upon Cain" — God set a mark or sign for Cain's protection (whether a visible mark on his person, or a sign given to him for assurance); the mark was not a brand of shame primarily, but a protective token, lest any finding him should kill him; the divine guarantee of Cain's preservation; God's mark shields the murderer's life. The verse displays the sevenfold vengeance threatened and the mark of Cain.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the protection — "whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold," God protecting Cain's life from private vengeance (Gill, Ellicott); the sevenfold threat a restraint, reserving vengeance to God, sparing the murderer (Calvin, Gill); "the LORD set a mark upon Cain," a protective sign, lest any kill him (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the marked Cain. The verse marks the vengeance: sevenfold. The verse displays the protection: of Cain's life. The verse displays the mark: set on him. The verse displays the restraint: vengeance reserved. The provision displays God protecting and marking the murderer. The verse displays the vengeance: threatened. The verse displays the mark: protective. The verse displays the Cain: preserved. The sevenfold vengeance threatened and the mark of Cain display God's forbearance toward the murderer: God threatened sevenfold vengeance on any who killed Cain and set a mark on him for his protection — sparing the murderer's life, reserving vengeance to himself, and preserving Cain to live out his cursed wandering. The verse displays the marked Cain, who departs from God's presence (v.16).
16:
"And Cain went out from the presence of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden." The witnesses note Cain departing from God's presence to the land of Nod. On "Cain went out from the presence of the LORD," the grammatical voices note the departure. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "Cain went out from the presence of the LORD" — Cain departed from the place of God's special presence (the place of worship, perhaps near Eden, where God manifested himself); the murderer leaves the presence and fellowship of God; whether a willful departure or the consequence of his sentence, Cain becomes an outcast from God; the alienation of the sinner from God is sealed.

On "from the presence of the LORD," the reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the alienation. Calvin and Henry note that Cain's going out from God's presence marks his estrangement; he leaves the worship and fellowship of God; the wicked, unrepentant, withdraw from God; Cain's departure is the sad picture of the apostate, cut off from God's presence.

On "dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden," the reformed JFB and grammatical-historical note the wandering land: Cain "dwelt in the land of Nod (which means 'wandering'), on the east of Eden" — Cain settled in the land of Nod, east of Eden; the name "Nod" (wandering) fits his sentence as a fugitive and vagabond; further east from Eden (the direction of increasing distance from God's garden); Cain's dwelling-place marks his outcast, wandering estate. The verse displays Cain departing from God's presence to the land of Nod.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the departure — "Cain went out from the presence of the LORD," Cain leaving the place of God's presence and fellowship (Keil, Henry); the going out the alienation of the unrepentant sinner from God (Calvin, Henry); "dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden," Cain settling in the land of "wandering," east of Eden, fitting his outcast sentence (JFB, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the outcast Cain. The verse marks the departure: from God's presence. The verse displays the alienation: of the sinner. The verse displays the land: Nod, wandering. The verse displays the place: east of Eden. The departure displays Cain estranged from God and dwelling in exile. The verse displays the Cain: departed. The verse displays the presence: forsaken. The verse displays the Nod: wandering. Cain departing from God's presence to the land of Nod displays the outcast estate of the murderer: Cain went out from the presence of the LORD and dwelt in the land of Nod, east of Eden — the alienation of the unrepentant sinner from God, settling in the land of "wandering," fitting his fugitive sentence. The verse displays the exiled Cain, who builds a city and founds a line (vv.17-24).
17:
"And Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch: and he builded a city, and called the name of the city, after the name of his son, Enoch." The witnesses note Cain's son Enoch and the building of the first city. On "Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch," the grammatical voices note Cain's family. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch" — Cain has a son, Enoch (a different Enoch from the godly Enoch of Seth's line, 5:18); Cain's wife was one of Adam's daughters (his sister or near kin, the race propagating from the first pair); the line of Cain begins.

On "he builded a city," the witnesses note the first city. Ellicott and Calvin note that Cain "builded a city" — the first city, built by Cain; whether a fortified settlement (Cain, the restless wanderer, seeking security and a settled place against his fear, v.14), or the beginning of urban civilization in Cain's line; the city marks the development of human society, but founded by the murderer and his line.

On "called the name of the city, after the name of his son, Enoch," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the naming: Cain "called the name of the city... Enoch" — naming the city after his son; whether to perpetuate his name and dynasty, or to establish a settled, secure life in defiance of his wandering sentence; Cain's line develops the arts of civilization (vv.20-22), yet apart from God; the city of Cain stands over against the people who call on the LORD (v.26). The verse displays Cain's son Enoch and the building of the first city.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the family — "Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch," Cain's son Enoch, the line of Cain beginning (Keil, Gill); "he builded a city," the first city, built by Cain for security or settlement (Ellicott, Calvin); "called the name of the city... Enoch," naming the city after his son, the development of civilization in Cain's line apart from God (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Cain's city. The verse marks the son: Enoch. The verse displays the city: first built. The verse displays the builder: Cain. The verse displays the line: of Cain. The verse displays Cain founding a city and a line apart from God. The verse displays the Enoch: born. The verse displays the city: named. The verse displays the civilization: begun. Cain's son Enoch and the building of the first city display the rise of Cain's line: Cain knew his wife, who bore Enoch, and he built a city and named it after his son — the line of Cain beginning, and the first city built, the development of human civilization, yet by the murderer's line apart from God. The verse displays Cain's line, traced to Lamech (vv.18-19).
18:
"And unto Enoch was born Irad: and Irad begat Mehujael: and Mehujael begat Methusael: and Methusael begat Lamech." The very brief verse notes the genealogy of Cain's line to Lamech. The grammatical voices note the descent. Gill and Ellicott note that "unto Enoch was born Irad: and Irad begat Mehujael: and Mehujael begat Methusael: and Methusael begat Lamech" — the genealogy traces Cain's line through five generations to Lamech; the descent from Cain through Enoch, Irad, Mehujael, Methusael, to Lamech; the line of the murderer's posterity is recorded.

On the names, the witnesses note the line. Gill and Ellicott note that the names mark the generations of Cain's line; the genealogy is brief, listing only the succession to Lamech, in whom the line's character (vv.19-24) is displayed; the descendants of Cain multiply and develop, but apart from God.

On the genealogy, the grammatical-historical note the contrast with Seth's line: the line of Cain (here) is set over against the line of Seth (ch. 5); the two genealogies trace the two seeds (3:15) — the line of Cain (the city-builders, the inventors, but godless) and the line of Seth (who call on the LORD, v.26); the genealogy of Cain leads to Lamech, the boastful avenger (vv.23-24), marking the line's moral decline. The verse displays the genealogy of Cain's line to Lamech.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the descent — "unto Enoch was born Irad... and Methusael begat Lamech," the genealogy of Cain's line through five generations to Lamech (Gill, Ellicott); the names marking the generations of the murderer's posterity (Gill, Ellicott); the line of Cain set over against Seth's, leading to the boastful Lamech (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Cain's genealogy. The verse marks the line: of Cain. The verse displays the generations: to Lamech. The verse displays the descent: through Enoch. The verse displays the contrast: with Seth's line. The genealogy displays the line of Cain traced to Lamech. The verse displays the Enoch: father of Irad. The verse displays the line: descending. The verse displays the Lamech: reached. The genealogy of Cain's line to Lamech displays the posterity of the murderer: from Enoch through Irad, Mehujael, and Methusael to Lamech — the line of Cain traced through five generations, the seed of the wicked developing apart from God, leading to the boastful Lamech. The verse displays Cain's line, in Lamech and his wives (v.19).
19:
"And Lamech took unto him two wives: the name of the one was Adah, and the name of the other Zillah." The brief verse notes Lamech taking two wives — the first polygamy. The grammatical voices note the departure from God's order. Gill and Henry note that "Lamech took unto him two wives: the name of the one was Adah, and the name of the other Zillah" — Lamech, of Cain's line, took two wives; this is the first recorded instance of polygamy; Lamech breaks the original ordinance of marriage (one man and one woman, one flesh, 2:24); the corruption of marriage in Cain's godless line.

On "took unto him two wives," the reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the corruption of marriage. Calvin and Henry note that Lamech's taking of two wives is a departure from God's institution of marriage (2:24, where the two are one flesh); polygamy, contrary to the creation ordinance, begins in Cain's line; the corruption of the marriage bond marks the moral decline of the ungodly seed; what God joined as one, fallen man multiplies.

On the two wives, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the line's character: the polygamy of Lamech, with his boastful violence (vv.23-24), marks the character of Cain's line — departing from God's order in marriage and in conduct; the ungodly line develops the arts (vv.20-22) but corrupts God's institutions; the contrast with the godly line of Seth. The verse displays Lamech taking two wives.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the polygamy — "Lamech took unto him two wives: the name of the one was Adah, and the name of the other Zillah," the first recorded polygamy, Lamech breaking the marriage ordinance (Gill, Henry); the corruption of marriage, contrary to God's institution (2:24, Calvin, Henry); the polygamy marking the moral decline of Cain's godless line (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the polygamist Lamech. The verse marks the wives: two. The verse displays the polygamy: first recorded. The verse displays the corruption: of marriage. The verse displays the line: godless. The deed displays Lamech corrupting the marriage ordinance. The verse displays the Lamech: polygamous. The verse displays the marriage: corrupted. The verse displays the line: declining. Lamech taking two wives displays the corruption of marriage in Cain's line: Lamech took two wives, Adah and Zillah — the first recorded polygamy, a departure from God's institution of marriage as one flesh (2:24), marking the moral decline of the ungodly seed. The verse displays Lamech's house, the seat of the early arts (vv.20-22).
20:
"And Adah bare Jabal: he was the father of such as dwell in tents, and of such as have cattle." The brief verse notes Jabal, father of tent-dwelling herdsmen. The grammatical voices note the pastoral art. Gill and Clarke note that "Adah bare Jabal: he was the father of such as dwell in tents, and of such as have cattle" — Jabal, son of Lamech, was the founder (father, originator) of the nomadic, pastoral way of life: dwelling in tents and keeping cattle; he developed the art of herding and the tent-dwelling, migratory life; the first of the herdsmen-nomads.

On "the father of such as dwell in tents, and of such as have cattle," the witnesses note the development of civilization. Calvin and Gill note that Jabal "was the father" — the originator or pattern — of the pastoral life; the arts of human society (herding, here; music and metalwork, vv.21-22) develop in Cain's line; the cultural advances of mankind arise, even in the godless line; God's gifts of skill and invention are spread, though the line is apart from God.

On the pastoral life, the grammatical-historical note the contrast: the arts and civilization advance in Cain's line (Jabal the herdsman, Jubal the musician, Tubal-cain the metalworker) — the development of human culture; yet this progress, in the line of Cain, is apart from God and accompanied by moral decline (Lamech's polygamy and violence); the gifts of civilization and the absence of godliness coexist in the seed of Cain. The verse displays Jabal, father of tent-dwelling herdsmen.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the pastoral art — "Adah bare Jabal: he was the father of such as dwell in tents, and of such as have cattle," Jabal the founder of the nomadic, pastoral life (Gill, Clarke); "the father of such as dwell in tents," the originator of the herding and tent-dwelling way (Calvin, Gill); the arts of civilization developing in Cain's line, apart from God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the herdsman Jabal. The verse marks the son: Jabal. The verse displays the art: herding. The verse displays the life: tent-dwelling. The verse displays the civilization: developing. The figure displays Jabal founding the pastoral, nomadic life. The verse displays the Jabal: father of herdsmen. The verse displays the tents: dwelt in. The verse displays the cattle: kept. Jabal, father of tent-dwelling herdsmen, displays the rise of the pastoral art in Cain's line: Jabal was the father of those who dwell in tents and keep cattle — the originator of the nomadic, pastoral life, the arts of human civilization developing in Cain's line, though apart from God. The verse displays Jabal's craft, beside his brother Jubal's music (v.21).
21:
"And his brother's name was Jubal: he was the father of all such as handle the harp and organ." The very brief verse notes Jubal, father of music. The grammatical voices note the musical art. Gill and Clarke note that "his brother's name was Jubal: he was the father of all such as handle the harp and organ" — Jubal, brother of Jabal, was the founder (father, originator) of music: the inventor or first master of the harp (a stringed instrument) and the organ (a wind/pipe instrument); he developed the art of music; the first of the musicians.

On "the father of all such as handle the harp and organ," the witnesses note the invention of music. Gill and Clarke note that Jubal "was the father" — the originator — of instrumental music; the harp and organ (representing stringed and wind instruments) mark the development of music as an art; the cultural gift of music arises in Cain's line; the arts of civilization (herding, music, metalwork) develop together.

On the music, the grammatical-historical note the place in the line's development: music, like the pastoral and metalworking arts (vv.20, 22), is a gift and skill developed in Cain's line; the arts advance, yet apart from God; the use of music may be for God's glory or, in the godless line, for pleasure and worldliness; the development of culture in the seed of Cain, marked by both skill and ungodliness. The verse displays Jubal, father of music.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the musical art — "his brother's name was Jubal: he was the father of all such as handle the harp and organ," Jubal the founder of music, the harp and organ (Gill, Clarke); "the father of all such as handle the harp and organ," the originator of instrumental music (Gill, Clarke); music a gift developed in Cain's line, apart from God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the musician Jubal. The verse marks the son: Jubal. The verse displays the art: music. The verse displays the instruments: harp and organ. The verse displays the civilization: developing. The figure displays Jubal founding the art of music. The verse displays the Jubal: father of musicians. The verse displays the harp: handled. The verse displays the organ: played. Jubal, father of music, displays the rise of the musical art in Cain's line: Jubal was the father of all who handle the harp and organ — the originator of instrumental music, the arts of civilization developing in Cain's line. The verse displays Jubal's art, beside Tubal-cain's metalwork (v.22).
22:
"And Zillah, she also bare Tubalcain, an instructer of every artificer in brass and iron: and the sister of Tubalcain was Naamah." The brief verse notes Tubal-cain, father of metalworking. The grammatical voices note the metal art. Gill and Clarke note that "Zillah... bare Tubalcain, an instructer of every artificer in brass and iron" — Tubal-cain, son of Lamech, was the founder of metalworking: the teacher and master of all who work in brass (copper/bronze) and iron; he developed the art of forging metals, making tools, weapons, and implements; the first of the smiths.

On "an instructer of every artificer in brass and iron," the witnesses note the development of metallurgy. Gill and JFB note that Tubal-cain "an instructer (sharpener, forger) of every artificer in brass and iron" — the originator of metalworking; the working of metals (brass and iron) marks a major advance in human civilization (tools, weapons, agriculture, war); the arts of metallurgy arise in Cain's line; the cultural gifts develop, though apart from God.

On "the sister of Tubalcain was Naamah," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the named sister: "the sister of Tubalcain was Naamah" — Naamah ("pleasant" or "lovely") is named, perhaps as a notable woman of the line; the naming of a woman is unusual in the genealogy; whether for her beauty, her notoriety, or some tradition, she is recorded; the line of Cain, with its arts and its women, is set down. The verse displays Tubal-cain, father of metalworking.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the metal art — "Zillah... bare Tubalcain, an instructer of every artificer in brass and iron," Tubal-cain the founder of metalworking, the master of smiths (Gill, Clarke); "an instructer of every artificer in brass and iron," the originator of metallurgy, a major advance in civilization (Gill, JFB); "the sister of Tubalcain was Naamah," the named woman of the line (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the smith Tubal-cain. The verse marks the son: Tubal-cain. The verse displays the art: metalworking. The verse displays the metals: brass and iron. The verse displays the sister: Naamah. The figure displays Tubal-cain founding the art of metallurgy. The verse displays the Tubal-cain: father of smiths. The verse displays the brass: worked. The verse displays the iron: forged. Tubal-cain, father of metalworking, displays the rise of metallurgy in Cain's line: Tubal-cain was the instructor of every worker in brass and iron, and his sister was Naamah — the originator of metalworking, the arts of civilization (herding, music, metallurgy) developed in Cain's line, apart from God. The verse displays the arts of Cain's line, beside Lamech's boast (vv.23-24).
23:
"And Lamech said unto his wives, Adah and Zillah, Hear my voice; ye wives of Lamech, hearken unto my speech: for I have slain a man to my wounding, and a young man to my hurt." The witnesses note Lamech's boastful song of violence. On "Lamech said unto his wives, Adah and Zillah, Hear my voice... hearken unto my speech," the grammatical voices note the boast. Gill and Henry note that "Lamech said unto his wives... Hear my voice; ye wives of Lamech, hearken unto my speech" — Lamech addresses his wives in a poetic, boastful speech (the first recorded poetry, a song of violence); he summons their attention to his vaunting words; the arrogant, self-glorying tone of the ungodly Lamech.

On "I have slain a man to my wounding, and a young man to my hurt," interpreters note the violence. Henry and Calvin note that "I have slain a man to my wounding (for wounding me), and a young man to my hurt (for hurting me)" — Lamech boasts of having killed a man (and a young man) in revenge for a wound; whether he killed in self-defense, or boasts of his readiness to kill any who cross him; the violence and vengefulness of Lamech, glorying in bloodshed; the moral decline of Cain's line, now boasting of murder.

On the boast, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the character: Lamech's song reveals the spirit of Cain's line — violent, vengeful, and boastful; in contrast to Cain, who feared retribution (4:14), Lamech glories in his power to avenge himself many times over (v.24); the swaggering pride of the godless seed; the corruption of the line that produced the arts also produces the boast of violence. The verse displays Lamech's boastful song of violence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the boast — "Lamech said unto his wives... Hear my voice... I have slain a man to my wounding," Lamech's boastful poetic song of violence (Gill, Henry); "I have slain a man... and a young man to my hurt," his boast of killing in revenge for a wound (Henry, Calvin); the song revealing the violent, vengeful spirit of Cain's line (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the boasting Lamech. The verse marks the speech: hear my voice. The verse displays the boast: I have slain. The verse displays the violence: vengeful. The verse displays the line: declining. The boast displays Lamech glorying in bloodshed. The verse displays the Lamech: boasting. The verse displays the slaying: avenged. The verse displays the pride: violent. Lamech's boastful song of violence displays the moral decline of Cain's line: Lamech boasted to his wives that he had slain a man for wounding him — a swaggering song of violence and vengeance, the godless line now glorying in bloodshed. The verse displays Lamech's boast, claiming a greater vengeance than Cain (v.24).
24:
"If Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and sevenfold." The very brief verse notes Lamech's boast of greater vengeance than Cain. The grammatical voices note the vaunt. Gill and Calvin note that "If Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and sevenfold" — Lamech, completing his boast (v.23), claims that if Cain's death would be avenged sevenfold (4:15), his own would be avenged seventy-sevenfold; he vaunts a far greater vengeance for himself; the swaggering self-assertion of the violent man.

On "If Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and sevenfold," the reformed Calvin notes the presumption. Calvin and Gill note that Lamech presumptuously applies God's word about Cain (the sevenfold vengeance, 4:15, which was God's protection of Cain) to his own case, magnifying it tenfold; he boasts that he is more terrible than Cain, more to be avenged; the arrogant abuse of God's word, turning divine forbearance into a boast of self-vengeance.

On the boast, the grammatical-historical note the contrast with Christ: Lamech's "seventy and sevenfold" of vengeance stands in striking contrast to Christ's "seventy times seven" of forgiveness (Matthew 18:22); the line of Cain boasts of unlimited vengeance, while the gospel calls for unlimited forgiveness; the spirit of Lamech (vengeance) and the spirit of Christ (forgiveness) are opposed; the moral character of Cain's line is sealed in this boast. The verse displays Lamech's boast of greater vengeance than Cain.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the vaunt — "If Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and sevenfold," Lamech boasting of a far greater vengeance for himself than Cain's (Gill, Calvin); the presumptuous abuse of God's word about Cain (4:15), turning it into a boast (Calvin, Gill); the contrast with Christ's "seventy times seven" of forgiveness (Matthew 18:22, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vaunting Lamech. The verse marks the boast: seventy-sevenfold. The verse displays the vengeance: magnified. The verse displays the presumption: abusing God's word. The verse displays the contrast: with forgiveness. The boast displays Lamech vaunting unlimited self-vengeance. The verse displays the Lamech: boasting. The verse displays the vengeance: claimed. The verse displays the spirit: violent. Lamech's boast of greater vengeance than Cain displays the climax of the godless line's pride: Lamech vaunted that if Cain were avenged sevenfold, he would be avenged seventy-sevenfold — boasting of unlimited self-vengeance, in striking contrast to the gospel's unlimited forgiveness. The verse displays the boastful Lamech, closing the line of Cain, before the birth of Seth (v.25).
25:
"And Adam knew his wife again; and she bare a son, and called his name Seth: For God, said she, hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel, whom Cain slew." The witnesses note the birth of Seth and the renewal of the godly line. On "Adam knew his wife again; and she bare a son, and called his name Seth," the grammatical voices note the new son. Ellicott and Gill note that "Adam knew his wife again; and she bare a son, and called his name Seth" — after the loss of Abel (slain) and the departure of Cain (cursed), Adam and Eve have another son, Seth (whose name relates to "appointed" or "set"); the birth of Seth begins a new, godly line (ch. 5), through which the promised seed would come.

On "God... hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel, whom Cain slew," the reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note Eve's faith. Calvin and Henry note that Eve names her son Seth, saying, "God hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel, whom Cain slew" — she acknowledges God's gift of another seed to replace the murdered Abel; her words express faith and hope: God has not let the godly seed fail, but has appointed another in Abel's place; the promise (3:15) continues through Seth; Eve's faith lays hold of God's provision.

On "another seed instead of Abel," the grammatical-historical note the godly line: Seth replaces Abel as the head of the godly line (the seed of the woman, in contrast to the line of Cain); through Seth and his descendants (ch. 5) the line of promise continues to Noah, and ultimately to Christ; God preserves a godly seed despite the murder of Abel and the apostasy of Cain; the line of redemption is secured in Seth. The verse displays the birth of Seth and the renewal of the godly line.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the new son — "Adam knew his wife again; and she bare a son, and called his name Seth," the birth of Seth, beginning a new godly line (Ellicott, Gill); "God... hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel," Eve's faith, God replacing the murdered Abel with another seed (Calvin, Henry); Seth the head of the godly line through which the promise continues (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the appointed Seth. The verse marks the son: Seth. The verse displays the appointment: another seed. The verse displays the replacement: of Abel. The verse displays the line: godly. The birth displays God appointing another seed in Abel's place. The verse displays the Seth: born. The verse displays the seed: appointed. The verse displays the promise: continued. The birth of Seth and the renewal of the godly line display God's preservation of the promise: Eve bore Seth, saying God had appointed her another seed instead of slain Abel — the godly line renewed in Seth, through whom the seed of promise would continue. The verse displays the appointed Seth, in whose line men call on the LORD (v.26).
26:
"And to Seth, to him also there was born a son; and he called his name Enos: then began men to call upon the name of the LORD." The witnesses note the birth of Enos and the beginning of calling on the LORD — the crux of the phrase. On "to Seth... there was born a son; and he called his name Enos," the grammatical voices note Seth's son. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "to Seth... there was born a son; and he called his name Enos" — Seth has a son, Enos (whose name means "man," especially man as frail, mortal); the godly line continues through Seth to Enos; the line of promise is traced.

On "then began men to call upon the name of the LORD," interpreters divide over the meaning. The grammatical-historical Keil and the reformed Calvin and Gill read it as the beginning of public worship and the invocation of God: men (in Seth's line) began to call upon (invoke, worship, pray to) the name of the LORD — the public, corporate worship of God began, the godly distinguishing themselves by their worship; the rise of religious assembly and prayer. A second reading (the margin/alternative) takes it as "then began men to be called by the name of the LORD" — the godly began to be called the sons of God, distinguished by his name. A third reading (the Jewish/Targumic) renders it negatively, "then began profanation" — the beginning of idolatry, men beginning to profane the name or to make idols/worship the stars.

On the phrase, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the worship-reading: Clarke and the dominant Christian tradition read it of the beginning of public worship — the godly line of Seth began the open, public invocation and worship of the LORD, in contrast to the godless line of Cain; the rise of true religion and worship in Enos's day. The verse displays the birth of Enos and the beginning of calling on the LORD.

The witnesses agree the substance: Seth's son Enos was born, and in his day men began to call upon the name of the LORD — the godly line marked by the worship of God, in contrast to Cain's line (Keil, Calvin, Gill, Clarke). The phrase bears a crux: the beginning of public worship/invocation of God (the common Christian reading — Keil, Calvin, Clarke) vs began to be called by the name of the LORD (the godly distinguished as God's people) vs (Jewish) then profanation/idolatry began. The verse displays the calling-on-the-LORD crux. The verse marks the son: Enos. The verse displays the debate: public worship, or being called by God's name, or profanation. The verse displays the line: godly. The verse displays the worship: begun. The birth displays the godly line marked by calling on the LORD. The verse displays the Enos: born. The verse displays the name: called upon. The verse displays the worship: public. The birth of Enos and the beginning of calling on the LORD display the rise of true worship in the godly line and a crux: Seth's son Enos was born, and then men began to call upon the name of the LORD — read by the common Christian view as the beginning of public worship (Keil, Calvin, Clarke), by an alternative as the godly being called by God's name, and by the Jewish reading as the beginning of profanation. The verse closes the chapter, the godly line of Seth set over against the line of Cain (ch.5ff.).
